title: Those Who Contest for Kazhagam Office and Win, and Those Who Miss the Opportunity to Win, Are Forever My Udanpirappugal!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The Tamil below is a source-checked reading copy; the frozen source chapter remains unchanged and authoritative. A duplicate OCR-corrupted rendering of the title that appeared between the title and salutation has been removed. Unambiguous OCR character errors and accidental word or page-break splits have been repaired without modernising the source. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: The fourteenth organisational election of our Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam is now under way. When the Kazhagam was founded on 17 September 1949, the draft constitution prepared by the Rules Committee established at the time was duly accepted by the General Council. It was then adopted at the first State Conference, held in Chennai in December 1951 under the leadership of Perarignar Anna. Since then, amendments proposed from time to time by Rules Amendment Committees in response to changing needs have been placed before the General Council, debated and duly adopted.

Thirteen organisational elections have so far been conducted in the Kazhagam, proceeding through the branch, town panchayat, union, city, district and headquarters organisations. The fourteenth is now in progress. Looking at the dates of the headquarters elections, the first was held in 1949; the second on 4 April 1955; the third on 25 September 1960; the fourth on 27 July 1969; the fifth on 7 July 1974; the sixth on 18 June 1978; the seventh on 26 May 1983; the eighth on 2 February 1988; the ninth on 2 June 1992; the tenth on 27 July 1997; the eleventh on 8 April 2000; the twelfth on 2 June 2003; and the thirteenth on 27 December 2008.

The Kazhagam has 84,36,744 members in this fourteenth organisational election. There were 62 lakh members at the tenth organisational election in 1997. The figure rose to 73 lakh for the eleventh election in 2000 and has now reached nearly 85 lakh for the fourteenth. Previously, union Kazhagams functioned as the organisation directly above the branch Kazhagams. Now, 12,514 panchayat Kazhagams have been announced as a new level between these organisations, and their elections are being conducted.

Some changes have been made between the thirteenth organisational election and the present fourteenth election. In particular, at the thirteenth election there were 36 Kazhagam districts, including the Madurai and Coimbatore metropolitan districts. For administrative convenience, those districts have now been divided into 65 Kazhagam districts. Likewise, the previous 392 union Kazhagams have now been divided into 578. There are also 129 city Kazhagams, 97 area Kazhagams and 538 town panchayat Kazhagams. Taking ward branches into account, the Kazhagam has 1,12,247 wards: 820 corporation wards, 8,397 town panchayat wards, 3,735 city wards and 99,295 panchayat wards across the 12,514 panchayats. Elections for the panchayat, city and town panchayat wards have been completed. Elections are continuing in the corporation wards. Elections for the union and city organisations are also now under way.

Even before announcing which union and city Kazhagams would fall within the boundaries of the newly constituted district Kazhagams, details of the Assembly constituencies forming each of the 65 districts were announced on 16 June 2014. The newspapers have likewise announced which unions and cities will be included in each district.

Until the Kazhagam’s thirteenth general election, membership cards were issued on the basis of names submitted by those in the branch organisations. But for this fourteenth Kazhagam election, when a member is enrolled, the membership form must contain the voter identity-card number issued by the Election Commission of India, along with the person’s name and other particulars. A photocopy of that voter card must also be attached. This time, membership cards have been issued only to those who attached such a copy. Even when a voter card was attached, a copy of an old voter card was not accepted. A membership card was issued only when a copy of the newly issued government Election Commission identity card was provided.

Taking advantage of technological progress, the membership card issued for the fourteenth election follows a modern “smart card” format. It records the member’s name, age, father’s or husband’s name, branch, panchayat, union, city or area, district, membership number and voter identity-card number. Bearing the signatures of the President and General Secretary, it was delivered directly to every member by headquarters representatives. The new cards for this election were issued to me, the General Secretary and the Treasurer on 29 October 2012. They were then distributed systematically in the districts from 1 November 2012 to 5 April 2013. Elections for ward branches and circle branches under the panchayat Kazhagams began on 15 April 2013 and continued until 31 December 2013, after which the list of panchayat Kazhagams was announced.

Nominations for panchayat Kazhagam elections were filed in four phases, and nominations for town panchayat Kazhagam elections were likewise filed in four phases. Continuing that sequence, the nomination dates for the union and city Kazhagam elections have now been announced and the relevant statements issued.

Just as the Chief Election Commission supplies an electoral roll for a general election, a Kazhagam electoral roll containing every detail on the membership card was supplied for the ward sub-branch elections. Those elections were conducted on that basis.

The commissioners sent from headquarters must perform their duty knowing that they are the trusted representatives of headquarters. They must also remember the fundamental truth that both sides contesting administrative office belong to the Kazhagam. They are contesting because they wish to accept the responsibility of working for the Kazhagam for the next five years. The candidate who secures the votes of the majority of members must be elected.
Why do I underline this? Because everyone must remain extremely vigilant and cooperate to ensure that not even a small violation of the rules occurs anywhere in this organisational election.

In an important headquarters announcement issued on 5 August 2014, we stated that anyone wishing to file a nomination who found themselves unable to submit it to the headquarters representatives could come directly to headquarters, obtain a nomination form, complete it and file it there. I trust that Kazhagam comrades have understood the purpose of this provision.

Headquarters, the General Secretary Professor, Treasurer Thalapathi and I do receive some letters of complaint about this election. In an election involving several lakhs of people in such a vast democratic movement, contests are only natural. In a people’s movement like the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam, it is understandable that several people will seek the same office and that the contest will consequently be intense. There are precedents for investigating and removing even minor errors that occurred despite our precautionary measures. Even now, whenever complaints arrive, arrangements have been made for two Organising Secretaries to conduct daily inquiries at headquarters and determine whether they are true.

In this party election, conducted democratically under the party’s constitution and rules, the Kazhagam leadership will certainly not act partially in anyone’s favour under any circumstances. Everyone contesting is a party member. The leadership’s approval will go only to those who democratically secure the highest number of votes and win. Those who miss the opportunity to win are also Kazhagam comrades. To win the next election, they must continue working tirelessly to earn the support of a majority of Kazhagam comrades. Everyone knows there is no shortcut within the Kazhagam.

Kazhagam members know that, like me, our General Secretary the Professor gives the highest importance to party discipline. They also know that Treasurer Thalapathi Stalin will not tolerate wrongdoing in any form, and that he attends to the organisational election every day, ensuring that the elections are conducted swiftly and properly. I therefore ask every Kazhagam Udanpirappu, with great affection, to extend the cooperation necessary to conduct this internal organisational election honestly and impartially, so that the Kazhagam may gain still greater strength and distinction.

The Kazhagam missed the opportunity for victory in the recently concluded parliamentary election. Why it lost is another matter. Yet even after a defeat of this scale, it is a source of happiness and pride that so many people are coming forward in group after group to seek office in the Kazhagam’s internal election and to contest.

Both sides contesting this organisational election are Kazhagam Udanpirappugal. The person who wins is my Udanpirappu, and the person who misses the opportunity to win is also my Udanpirappu! At the same time that I express happiness for the victor, I am also the one who offers comfort to those who did not win. I therefore ask every contestant to think and act in this spirit: if we have the support of a majority of Kazhagam comrades, we can win; if the opportunity slips away this time, we shall work with many times greater enthusiasm so that we win the next.

“The Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam is not merely a political party; it is a movement for social reform. For all those involved in it, the question is not who occupies which place; nor is it important who assumes which responsibility at which time. Each person’s acceptance of a responsibility is like taking a position in the battle camp constituted by this movement. If enemies try to enter it, they will be caught between our tightly joined ranks and crushed to pulp.

“The Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam is an organisation created so that each person may work from a particular position. If our opponents imagine that there are other hierarchies, resentments or jealousies among us, we can only say that they do not know the history of how we grew. The reason we were not born from one mother’s womb is simply that no one mother’s womb could have borne so many children!”
I want every Udanpirappu to keep in mind this deeply moving speech delivered by Perarignar Anna at a meeting on Chennai’s seashore on 26 September 1960.
With affection,
M.K.
5 September 2014
